skill.name: github-actions-templates
skill.description: Create production-ready GitHub Actions workflows for automated testing, building, and deploying applications. Use when setting up CI/CD with GitHub Actions, automating development workflows, or creating reusable pipeline templates.
skill.summary: Create production-ready GitHub Actions workflows for automated testing, building, and deploying applications with security best practices.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This skill is documentation-only containing GitHub Actions YAML workflow templates. All static analysis findings are false positives: the detected 'external_commands' are YAML run: syntax in markdown code blocks, 'network' references are URL configuration values, 'filesystem' patterns are reusable workflow references, and 'env_access' patterns are GitHub Actions secret syntax (${{ secrets.* }}). No executable code, no prompt injection attempts, and no security risks detected. The skill teaches legitimate DevOps practices including proper secret handling and secure workflow patterns.

content.value_statement: Teams struggle to create secure, production-ready GitHub Actions workflows from scratch. This skill provides tested templates for testing, building, and deploying applications with proper secret handling and approval gates.
actual_capabilities: Generate test workflow templates with matrix builds for multiple Node.js and Python versions
Create Docker build and push workflows with container registry authentication
Build Kubernetes deployment workflows with AWS EKS integration
Design reusable workflows for standardized testing across repositories
Implement security scanning workflows with Trivy and Snyk integration
Configure deployment workflows with environment approval gates
limitations: Templates require customization for specific repository structures and deployment targets
Secret names and configuration values must be adapted to your organization's setup
Cloud provider credentials (AWS, GCP, Azure) must be configured separately in GitHub Secrets
Self-hosted runner configuration requires additional infrastructure setup

use_cases.title: Startup CI/CD Setup
use_cases.description: Quickly establish professional testing and deployment pipelines for a new software project without deep GitHub Actions expertise.

use_cases.title: Enterprise Workflow Standardization
use_cases.description: Create consistent, secure workflow patterns across multiple repositories with reusable workflow templates and approval processes.

use_cases.title: Open Source Project Automation
use_cases.description: Set up automated testing across multiple operating systems and runtime versions to ensure broad compatibility.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Test Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a GitHub Actions workflow that runs tests on every pull request to the main branch. The project uses Node.js with npm. Include caching for dependencies and upload test coverage reports.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner: Setting up basic CI for a Node.js project

prompt_templates.title: Docker Build and Deploy
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a workflow that builds a Docker image on push to main, tags it with the git SHA and version tag, pushes to GitHub Container Registry, and deploys to a staging environment. Include proper permissions and secret handling.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Environment Deployment
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a deployment workflow with separate staging and production environments. Production deployments should require manual approval. Include Slack notifications for deployment success and failure, and rollback capability.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced: Production deployment with approval gates

prompt_templates.title: Security Scanning Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a comprehensive security workflow that runs Trivy for vulnerability scanning, Snyk for dependency checks, and CodeQL for code analysis. Upload all results to GitHub Security tab and fail the workflow on critical vulnerabilities.

output_examples.input: Create a test workflow for a Python project that runs on Ubuntu and macOS with Python 3.9 through 3.12
output_examples.output: A complete YAML workflow file with matrix strategy testing across 8 combinations (2 OS x 4 Python versions), including dependency caching, pytest execution, and coverage upload to Codecov.

output_examples.input: Build and push a Docker image when a new version tag is created
output_examples.output: A workflow that triggers on semantic version tags, logs into GitHub Container Registry using GITHUB_TOKEN, builds the image with versioned tags, and pushes with metadata labels for tracking.

best_practices: Pin action versions to specific major versions (e.g., @v4) instead of @latest or @main to prevent unexpected breaking changes and supply chain attacks
Use GitHub Secrets for all sensitive values including API keys, tokens, and credentials - never hardcode secrets in workflow files
Implement minimum required permissions using the permissions block and use environment protection rules with required reviewers for production deployments
anti_patterns: Using @latest or branch references for third-party actions which can introduce breaking changes or security vulnerabilities without notice
Storing credentials or tokens directly in workflow files or repository code instead of using GitHub Secrets for all sensitive values
Running untrusted code from pull requests from forks with write permissions which could expose secrets or compromise the CI environment

faq.question: How do I configure secrets for my GitHub Actions workflows?
faq.answer: Navigate to your repository Settings > Secrets and variables > Actions. Click 'New repository secret' and add your secret name and value. Reference secrets in workflows using ${{ secrets.SECRET_NAME }} syntax.

faq.question: What is the difference between jobs and steps in GitHub Actions?
faq.answer: Jobs are top-level workflow units that run on separate runners and can execute in parallel. Steps are individual commands or actions within a job that run sequentially on the same runner.

faq.question: How can I require approval before deploying to production?
faq.answer: Configure a protected environment in repository Settings > Environments. Add required reviewers to the production environment. Reference this environment in your workflow using the 'environment' key to enforce approval gates.

faq.question: Can I reuse workflows across multiple repositories?
faq.answer: Yes. Create reusable workflows in a central repository using the workflow_call trigger. Other repositories can call these workflows using the 'uses' keyword with the repository path and workflow filename.

faq.question: How do I cache dependencies to speed up workflows?
faq.answer: Use the actions/cache action or built-in caching in setup actions like actions/setup-node with the 'cache' input. Specify the dependency file hash as the cache key to invalidate cache when dependencies change.

faq.question: What are self-hosted runners and when should I use them?
faq.answer: Self-hosted runners are machines you manage that execute workflow jobs. Use them for sensitive workloads requiring internal network access, specialized hardware, or when GitHub-hosted runner limits are insufficient.
